title: Self-hosting music with Navidrome and Cloudflare Tunnel

content.text: To start, I created an 8GB + 1 core + 512MB of memory Debian 13 LXC container to host my music system. I decided to go with a Navidrome, as I read on the internet that it was more efficient, lighter and generally better than Jellyfin. As a first step, I had to configure the system (same as previous configurations so I will just leave commands below):

apt update && upgrade -y

apt install sudo

adduser sperek

usermod -aG sudo sperek
Then I downloaded and installed the software from the official repository:

wget https://github.com/navidrome/navidrome/releases/download/v0.61.1/navidrome_0.61.1_linux_amd64.deb

sudo apt install ./navidrome_0.61.1_linux_amd64.deb
Enabled the service and checked if it’s running correctly:

sudo systemctl enable --now navidrome

sudo systemctl status navidrome
sudo journalctl -u navidrome -f

sperek@navidrome:~$ sudo systemctl status navidrome
sudo journalctl -u navidrome -f
* navidrome.service - Your Personal Streaming Service
     Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/navidrome.service; enabled; preset: enabled)
     Active: active (running) since Thu 2026-04-09 10:34:15 UTC; 4s ago
That was incredibely quick and easy, everything working as expected. After setting up the service I wanted to connect it to my SAMBA drive for easy access and synchronization:

apt update && apt install cifs-utils -y

mkdir -p /mnt/muzyka

nano ~/.smbcredentials

username=USERNAME
password=PASSWORD
domain=WORKGROUP
However I had a problem with permissions, I was not able to get into it no matter what:

chmod 600 ~/.smbcredentials

sperek@navidrome:~$ sudo mount -t cifs -o credentials=/home/sperek/.smbcredentials,iochars
et=utf8,vers=3.0 //XXX.XXX.XXX.XXX/mininas/ /mnt/music
mount error(1): Operation not permitted
Refer to the mount.cifs(8) manual page (e.g. man mount.cifs) and kernel log messages (dmesg)
After playing with the configs I just gave up and connected my drive directly to the container. To keep things organised and safe, I mounted only one, previously created directory:
pct set 101 -mp0 /mnt/pve/ssd-samba/music,mp=/mnt/music
And set the new source path for Navidrome:

sudo nano /etc/navidrome/navidrome.toml

MusicFolder = "/mnt/music"

sudo systemctl restart navidrome

Another success! However I wanted to listen to my music library on a daily basis, not only in my local environment. I know I could have set up Tailscale and use VPN, but that was way too tedious for my usage. This is the part where Cloudflare reverse proxying came to the rescue. As I already had a DNS server for my website, I came up with an idea to use it for the Cloudflare Tunnel feature, which would allow me to connect to my service safely from anywhere I want (almost). After creating account I followed tutorial from their website to install their daemon:

sudo apt install curl

# Add cloudflare gpg key
sudo mkdir -p --mode=0755 /usr/share/keyrings
curl -fsSL https://pkg.cloudflare.com/cloudflare-public-v2.gpg | sudo tee /usr/share/keyrings/cloudflare-public-v2.gpg >/dev/null

# Add this repo to your apt repositories
echo 'deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/cloudflare-public-v2.gpg] https://pkg.cloudflare.com/cloudflared any main' | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/cloudflared.list

# install cloudflared
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install cloudflared
Then I connected my container with their service and that was it:
sudo cloudflared service install SUPERLONGHASHORSOMETHING

Of course it wasn’t that easy, this reverse proxy thing was definitely less pleasant than setting up navidrome. After creating the tunnel I had to replace OVH DNS servers with Cloudflare’s (as a proud European I have to admit, it was painful). Then I waited ~6 hours so that the new servers were correctly synchronized, but after that time it still didn't work. I did some digging and found that OVH scheduled these tasks for the next day, so I forced it to do that immediately and puffff… It worked! Yup, it worked but I did that on the break at work, so I was surprised that upon returning home I could not connect using my wifi. When I disabled wifi and used the 5G network it worked flawlessly, so I assumed that the name wasn’t propagated correctly and my internet operator had to refresh their servers. I checked the propagation of my domain name using a few websites and as I thought, it was okay in some places and could not connect in other ones. I’m still not sure if it was the case, but after a day or so, my local network finally caught up and it’s still working fine as of today.
